comments.body:
Progress update from the 2026-04-02 SitRep pass.

Local dossier artifacts created:
- `docs/Delivery/SitRep-2026-04-02/00-INDEX.md`
- `docs/Delivery/SitRep-2026-04-02/01-Findings-and-Evidence.md`
- `docs/Delivery/SitRep-2026-04-02/02-Timeline-and-Execution-Options.md`

Key evidence-backed findings so far:
- skill operational state is under-modelled: current catalog/readiness paths conflate loaded/installed with operational
- `graphenterprise` appears to have a real connector/auth path, but tenant/bootstrap readiness is not truthfully represented to the user
- quoted/follow-up continuity remains architecturally brittle: quoted context is present in prompt construction, but I do not yet see it elevated into strong deterministic routing state
- the app likely has maturity debt in its user-facing honesty surfaces (status badges, readiness labels, discovery-vs-execution transitions)

Backlog outputs created from this pass:
- `#484` О“ГҮГ¶ Distinguish loaded/installed from operational state in Skills Library and skill readiness UX
- `#485` О“ГҮГ¶ Follow-up skill verification can drift into health/discovery prose instead of execution proof

Still in progress:
- broader docs/issues/delivery timeline mapping
- review of remaining open workstreams and reprioritization options
- linkage/recommendation pass back into the backlog

Second/third-pass SitRep update.

New local artifacts added under `docs/Delivery/SitRep-2026-04-02/`:
- `04-Issue-Thread-Deep-Dive.md`
- `05-Codepath-Deep-Dive.md`
- `06-Claims-vs-Reality-Matrix.md`

Second-pass refinements:
- several closed issues were narrower and more honest than a first glance suggested (notably `#376`, `#408`, `#431`, `#465`)
- the strongest current drift remains at the **product edge**, especially:
  - skill operational-state truthfulness (`#484`)
  - follow-up execution-proof drift after status/discovery answers (`#485`)
- `graphenterprise` appears to be a real repo-side integration slice with actual connector/auth/test coverage; the misleading part is current readiness/availability semantics, not that the integration is fake
- the old quoted-clarification ack-stranding bug from `#431` appears genuinely fixed; current continuity concerns look broader and more downstream than that original seam
- some discovery-to-action paths are healthy again (see the `#400` closeout history), so the current routing/execution problem looks selective rather than universal

Third-pass synthesis:
- architecture still looks broadly on-course
- many narrow delivery slices are real
- the main maturity debt is that user-facing honesty and execution-proof behavior have not kept up with the complexity of the underlying system

Current strongest recommendation remains a stability-and-honesty correction wave before further broad surface expansion.

comments.body: Additional debug clue: the debug messages in the screenshot have no correlation IDs, which makes it much harder to trace the failing request end-to-end.

Shipped a two-commit fix sequence for the `#480` failure family and revalidated it live on the deployed bot.

## What changed

Commits on `main`:
- `4f82543` О“ГҮГ¶ `fix(#480): restore verbose failover telemetry`
- `f144b8c` О“ГҮГ¶ `fix(#480): prefer reliable default lane`

Repo-grounded changes:
- `src/orchestrator/turnTelemetry.ts`
  - correlation id is now present even in minimal-mode formatting paths
  - verbose footer can now include a model sequence chain when multiple model hops occur
- `src/orchestrator/sessionOrchestrator.ts`
  - initial exact non-core tools still bypass the LLM when appropriate
  - final footer now reflects the latest/final model used across follow-up hops
  - runtime footer emission is forced to verbose during the current dev/debug phase so trace data is always present
- `src/llm/foundryClient.ts`
  - failover now skips models already marked down by aggregate health tracking
  - successful fallback responses now report the final deployment model rather than the failed original slot
- `src/llm/llmHealthTracker.ts`
  - exposes per-model О“ГҮВЈtracked downО“ГҮВҘ state for routing decisions
- `src/llm/modelRouter.ts`
  - default unlabeled global prompts now front through the reliable fast lane instead of the flaky Grok default slot during active development

## Local validation
- focused suite + build О“ВЈГ 
- full serialized suite О“ВЈГ 
- final full result: `130` files / `466` tests passed

## Deploy evidence
- `Deploy Stamp` run `23886513832` О“ВЈГ 
- `Deploy Stamp` run `23887564380` О“ВЈГ 

## Live validation

### 1) Plain unlabeled Outlook search prompt
Prompt:
- `Search my Outlook inbox for emails with attachments. Return only the top 3 message ids, subjects, and receivedAt values as compact JSON.`

Observed reply:
- succeeded on the default lane
- returned mailbox JSON
- footer now included:
  - `corr:61bbfc6f`
  - verbose span breakdown
  - tool list
  - model chain
  - final model `m:gpt-5.4-mini-2026-03-17`
- operational note also correctly stated:
  - `o4-mini hit a 429 quota/rate limit; auto-failed over to gpt-5.4-mini and continued your request.`

This addresses both of the debug regressions:
- missing correlation ids in the footer
- footer showing the wrong model after failover

### 2) The exact `#480` repro prompt
Prompt:
- `can you find your own azure resource group and list the resources inside`

Observed final reply:
- `I canО“ГҮГ–t access Azure right now because authentication to the Azure MCP tools is failing, so I canО“ГҮГ–t list any resource groups or resources.`
- footer included full trace data and `corr:124f339b`

That is materially different from the original bug report. The bot no longer returns:
- `О“ГңГЎвҲ©в••Г… Operational note: the model provider returned an unexpected error. Please retry in a minute.`

Instead it now produces a concrete, actionable blocker tied to Azure auth/tool access.

## Conclusion
This issue can close as fixed:
- the generic model-provider failure is gone for the reproduced prompt
- correlation ids are back in the debug footer
- verbose debug telemetry is restored for current development needs
- default unlabeled prompts no longer depend on the unhealthy Grok front-door slot
- failover reporting now surfaces the actual final model path

## Remaining note
The Azure lookup itself is still blocked by Azure MCP authentication/runtime access, but that is now surfaced honestly as a specific Azure-auth/tool-access problem rather than a misleading model-provider failure.

comments.body:
This residual bug is now resolved by the later follow-up fixes that landed after `e775002`.

## Resolution chain

The final fix was not just one patch. The shipped sequence was:

- `fb87091` О“ГҮГ¶ semantic normalization for duplicate email fingerprints
- `2719d9b` О“ГҮГ¶ handler-level idempotency at the actual Outlook `sendMail` boundary
- `64dfdfd` О“ГҮГ¶ clarified duplicate-suppression wording so the bot does not imply the original send failed
- `7eb1a09` О“ГҮГ¶ documented the reusable outbound idempotency primitive for future skills

## Why this closes `#471`

This issue specifically described the residual secondary-lane duplicate send that survived `e775002`.

That exact failure mode was re-tested after the later fixes.

### Earlier failing state (the evidence that opened this bug)
- subject: `DL-dupcheck-secondary-e775002`
- sent-items verification reply: `Exact subject appears twice.`

### Final passing state after later fixes
Human send probe:
> `/light Use the exact tool outlook_send_email ... subject "DL-dupcheck-secondary-64dfdfd" ...`

Bot reply:
> `Yes, Mr. Anderson О“ГҮГ¶ the email was actually sent.`

Mailbox verification probe for the same exact subject:
> inspect sentitems and count exact subject `DL-dupcheck-secondary-64dfdfd`

Bot reply:
> `Exactly one email matches.`

That satisfies the core acceptance criterion for this issue on the lane that was still failing.

## Additional grounding

The durable architectural fix was to stop relying only on planner/dispatch duplicate suppression and instead protect the real side-effect boundary in:
- `skills/outlook/handlers.ts`
- backed by the shared outbound-artifact claim store in `src/bot/conversationStore.ts`

So this residual bug is no longer open work; it was fixed during the `#439` follow-up investigation and validated live against the mailbox.

Closing because the residual secondary-lane duplicate-send bug was fixed and live-validated by the later follow-up patches (fb87091, 2719d9b, 64dfdfd). The previously failing exact-subject mailbox count is now 1, not 2.

comments.body:
Shipped and deployed a real fix for `#470`.

## What changed

Commit:
- `efe1cae` О“ГҮГ¶ `fix(#470): harden sent items outlook search`

Deployed via:
- `Deploy Stamp` run `23859528747` О“ВЈГ 

Repo-grounded implementation:
- `skills/outlook/handlers.ts`
  - kept the normal Graph `$search` path
  - added a fallback recent-message scan when `$search` returns no hits
  - fallback filters locally against real message fields (`from`, `toRecipients`, `ccRecipients`, `subject`, `hasAttachments`)
  - this is what fixes folder-scoped Sent Items recipient false negatives
- `skills/outlook/manifest.json`
  - exposed the optional `folder` parameter on `outlook_search_emails` so the live tool contract can explicitly target `sentitems`
- tests added/updated:
  - `tests/skills/outlookSearchEmail.test.ts`
  - `tests/skills/outlookManifest.test.ts`

## Local validation

Focused:
- `pnpm test -- tests/skills/outlookSearchEmail.test.ts tests/skills/outlookManifest.test.ts tests/skills/outlookAttachments.test.ts tests/skills/outlookReplyToLatestEmail.test.ts` О“ВЈГ 
- `pnpm build` О“ВЈГ 

Repo-wide:
- `pnpm test -- --no-file-parallelism --maxWorkers 1` О“ВЈГ 
- result: `121` test files, `432` tests passed

## Live deployed validation

### Setup step: create a known attachment-bearing sent message
To avoid validating against mailbox folklore, I created a fresh runtime asset and then a fresh attachment-bearing sent message.

Relevant live results:
- `/assetingest selftest secondary`
  - returned runtime asset ids including:
    - `asset-ingest-selftest.txt` О“ГҘГҶ `84ef89e3-505a-4f3c-af43-942f11428326`
- a new sent message with subject:
  - `DL-470-attachment-send`

### Primary lane (`/heavy`) О“ГҮГ¶ Sent Items recipient search now returns truthful hits
Probe:
> `/heavy Use the exact tool outlook_search_emails with folder sentitems, query "eric@eanderson.de", and top 5...`

Observed reply included:
- `DL-470-attachment-send`
- `hasAttachments: true`

Footer:
- `E2E:32695ms`
- model lane `o4m` with quota failover notice

This is the key fix proof for the original bug: Sent Items recipient search no longer returns a false empty result.

### Secondary lane (`/light`) О“ГҮГ¶ attachment-filtered Sent Items search works
Probe:
> `/light Use the exact tool outlook_search_emails with folder sentitems, query "eric@eanderson.de hasAttachment:true", and top 3...`

Observed reply:
```json
{"results":[{"id":"AAMkAGI1NDcyNTkzLTVhMGUtNDI3ZC1iN2FjLTg1ODAwN2E1YWJiZgBGAAAAAACc4hKeUcblT5FLq6otPWlYBwDh_1pqoTo-SqsDGdmpBXtMAAAAAAEJAADh_1pqoTo-SqsDGdmpBXtMAAAMTRWFAAA=","subject":"DL-470-attachment-send","hasAttachments":true}]}
```

Footer:
- `E2E:7642ms`
- model `gpt-5.4-mini-2026-03-17`

### Secondary lane (`/light`) О“ГҮГ¶ attachment inspection on the returned sent message works
Probe used the returned message id with `outlook_list_attachments`.

Observed reply:
```json
{"bodyCidReferences":[],"attachments":[{"id":"AAMkAGI1NDcyNTkzLTVhMGUtNDI3ZC1iN2FjLTg1ODAwN2E1YWJiZgBGAAAAAACc4hKeUcblT5FLq6otPWlYBwDh_1pqoTo-SqsDGdmpBXtMAAAAAAEJAADh_1pqoTo-SqsDGdmpBXtMAAAMTRWFAAABEgAQAJ-iKyLLt3tJiBePEEgSXII=","name":"asset-ingest-selftest.txt","attachmentKind":"file-download","downloadSupported":true}]}
```

Footer:
- `E2E:7146ms`
- model `gpt-5.4-mini-2026-03-17`

## Conclusion
This issue was about the broken/false-negative Sent Items search path blocking attachment verification.

That path is now honestly proven working on deployed behavior:
- primary lane can find Sent Items messages for `eric@eanderson.de`
- secondary lane can filter those results down to `hasAttachment:true`
- secondary lane can inspect the found sent message's attachment metadata

So the observed failure mode from the screenshot is resolved.

Closing on shipped + deployed evidence from commit efe1cae / deploy run 23859528747. Primary lane now finds truthful Sent Items recipient hits, and secondary lane can both filter to hasAttachment:true and inspect the found sent message attachments.

comments.body:
Shipped a real first slice for `#463` and validated it locally plus live after deploy.

## What shipped

Commit:
- `684c68a` О“ГҮГ¶ `feat(#463): add capability-group discovery slices`

Deploy:
- `Deploy Stamp` run `23862424903` О“ВЈГ 

## Repo-grounded implementation

### Manifest/schema layer
- `src/capabilities/manifestSchema.ts`
  - added manifest-level `capabilityGroups`
  - added tool-level `capabilityGroup`
  - validates that a tool cannot reference an undefined capability group

### Discovery index
- `src/capabilities/skillDiscoveryIndex.ts`
  - added a first-class `capabilityGroups` tier between skills and tools
  - search now returns group hits as well as skill/tool hits
  - added `getDiscoveryCapabilityGroup()` for downstream lookups

### Execution narrowing
- `src/orchestrator/discoveryToolInjection.ts`
  - selective follow-up injection now honors matched capability groups, not just explicit tool hits or whole-skill recommended entry tools
  - read-only discovery responses can name the matched capability group directly

### Core discovery tool
- `skills/core/handlers.ts`
- `skills/core/manifest.json`
  - `helkin_skill_search` now supports:
    - `describe_group`
    - `list_groups`
  - `search` now returns capability-group results in addition to skill/tool matches

### Live reference skill
- `skills/outlook/manifest.json`
  - added concrete capability groups:
    - `outlook/mail-read`
    - `outlook/mail-write`
    - `outlook/calendar`
  - mapped Outlook tools into those groups
  - this gives the shipped runtime a real non-synthetic reference case for the new middle tier

## Local validation

Focused:
- `pnpm test -- tests/capabilities/skillDiscoveryIndex.test.ts tests/skills/coreSkillSearch.test.ts tests/orchestrator/discoveryToolInjection.test.ts tests/skills/outlookManifest.test.ts` О“ВЈГ 
- `pnpm build` О“ВЈГ 
- focused result: `4` files / `30` tests passed

Repo-wide:
- `pnpm test -- --no-file-parallelism --maxWorkers 1` О“ВЈГ 
- full result: `121` files / `435` tests passed

## Live deployed validation

### Secondary lane (`/light`) О“ГҮГ¶ capability groups are discoverable in search
Probe:
> `/light Use the exact tool helkin_skill_search with command "search" and query "inspect outlook sent items attachments"...`

Observed reply included:
```json
{"capabilityGroups":[{"id":"outlook/mail-read","displayName":"Mail Read Operations","toolCount":5},{"id":"outlook/calendar","displayName":"Calendar Operations","toolCount":2},{"id":"outlook/mail-write","displayName":"Mail Write Operations","toolCount":2}], ...}
```

Footer:
- `E2E:12658ms`
- model `gpt-5.4-mini-2026-03-17`

This proves the new middle tier is visible in the shipped discovery surface.

### Primary lane (`/heavy`) О“ГҮГ¶ describe_group returns the narrowed subset and upstream namespace
Probe:
> `/heavy Use the exact tool helkin_skill_search with command "describe_group" and groupId "outlook/mail-read"...`

Observed reply:
```json
{"groupId":"outlook/mail-read","domain":"outlook","displayName":"Mail Read Operations","upstreamNamespace":"mail.read","toolNames":["outlook_list_emails","outlook_read_email","outlook_list_attachments","outlook_download_attachment","outlook_search_emails"],"toolCount":5}
```

Footer:
- `E2E:11890ms`
- model lane `o4m`

This proves the runtime can distinguish the parent skill (`outlook`) from a concrete capability group and its narrowed tool family.

## Why this closes `#463`

This issue asked for a practical first slice of granular namespace/sub-tool loading, not a fantasy О“ГҮВЈall Azure at onceО“ГҮВҘ dump.

The shipped slice now provides:
- skill / capability-group / tool representation
- discovery results that include the middle tier
- group-aware follow-up narrowing in orchestration
- explicit upstream namespace metadata for MCP-backed/high-fanout skill families
- a concrete shipped reference case (`outlook`) proving the design works on a live installed skill

This composes with future MCP-backed work instead of baking Azure-only special cases into the platform.

Closing on shipped + deployed evidence from commit 684c68a / deploy run 23862424903. Capability-group discovery is now live, search surfaces the middle tier, and describe_group returns a narrowed Outlook tool family with upstream namespace metadata.

comments.body:
Shipped a real first slice for `#450`: HelkinSwarm now has a manifest-driven stdio MCP connector that can register MCP-backed tools through the normal capability loader / tool registry / handler path.

## What shipped

Commits:
- `a2bef5a` О“ГҮГ¶ `feat(#450): add stdio mcp connector`
- `14f4bc6` О“ГҮГ¶ `fix(#450): sync lockfile for mcp runtime sdk`

(There was one failed intermediate deploy run `23863981053` caused by a frozen-lockfile mismatch after promoting `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk` to a runtime dependency. That was fixed by `14f4bc6`.)

## Repo-grounded implementation

### Manifest/schema layer
- `src/capabilities/manifestSchema.ts`
  - added optional top-level `mcpServer` config
    - current v1 transport support: `stdio`
  - added optional tool-level `remoteToolName`
  - kept HelkinSwarm manifest metadata authoritative for risk / sensitivity / privilege / schemas

### Runtime connector layer
- `src/mcp/mcpConnector.ts`
  - creates stdio MCP clients with `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk`
  - validates the remote tool surface against the manifest-declared tools
  - registers MCP-backed handlers through the existing handler registry
  - enforces timeout handling and surfaces initialization/call failures cleanly

### Capability loader integration
- `src/capabilities/capabilityLoader.ts`
  - now initializes MCP-backed handlers when a manifest declares `mcpServer`
  - this means MCP-backed tools still arrive through the normal manifest-loader / tool-registry path rather than any bespoke orchestrator shortcut

### Reference MCP-backed skill
- `skills/mcpreference/manifest.json`
- `skills/mcpreference/server.mjs`
  - added a small standard stdio MCP server and matching manifest-driven skill wrapper
  - remote tools exposed through HelkinSwarm:
    - `mcpreference_echo`
    - `mcpreference_uppercase`

### Runtime dependency scope
- `package.json`
- `pnpm-lock.yaml`
  - moved `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk` into production dependencies so the deployed container can actually run the connector and the reference MCP server

comments.body:
Shipped a real first slice for `#449`: HelkinSwarm now has a first-party, read-only MCP Registry discovery tool backed by a synced local in-process catalog cache rather than live registry round-trips for every prompt.

## What shipped

Commit:
- `d3bc06c` О“ГҮГ¶ `feat(#449): add mcp registry discovery cache`

Successful deploy:
- `Deploy Stamp` run `23866103132` О“ВЈГ 

## Repo-grounded implementation

### New local registry catalog
- `src/mcp/mcpRegistryCatalog.ts`
  - fetches the official MCP Registry from `GET /v0.1/servers`
  - requests `version=latest` so search results are candidate/latest-version oriented
  - handles cursor pagination
  - uses incremental sync via `updated_since` after the first full sync
  - keeps a process-local synced cache on an hourly cadence instead of hitting the registry every prompt
  - validates inbound registry data with Zod at the boundary
  - drops malformed candidates that lack usable package/transport metadata
  - preserves `status` values (`active`, `deprecated`, `deleted`) in the local catalog

### New core discovery tool
- `skills/core/handlers.ts`
- `skills/core/manifest.json`
  - added `helkin_mcp_registry_search`
  - commands shipped:
    - `help`
    - `search`
    - `status`
    - `refresh`
  - keeps external registry discovery explicitly separate from installed-skill discovery (`helkin_skill_search`)
  - returns downstream-evaluation-shaped candidate metadata:
    - server name
    - title / description
    - latest version
    - package + transport metadata
    - status / status message
    - repository URL / homepage when present

### Telemetry
- `src/observability/telemetry.ts`
  - added explicit event names for MCP Registry refresh/search activity

## Local validation

- `pnpm build` О“ВЈГ

- pagination + local cache sync
- malformed candidate drop behavior
- deleted candidate handling
- core tool help/search/status/refresh contract

## Why this closes `#449`

Additional design note captured as follow-on issue: #453

Important distinction to preserve in this epic:

The MCP Registry is not only a source of directly onboardable third-party MCP servers. It is also a valuable source of implementation ideas, tool-shape patterns, auth flows, UX concepts, and edge cases that can accelerate **first-party native HelkinSwarm skills**.

Concrete example:
- WhatsApp is already on the HelkinSwarm radar.
- There is an existing MCP implementation at `https://github.com/meharajM/whatsapp-mcp`.
- That does **not** automatically mean we should onboard it directly.
- It may be better to study its concepts/tool surface (`connect`, `disconnect`, `send_message`, `ask_question`, `get_status`), QR-based auth flow, allowed-number restrictions, and HITL WhatsApp interaction model О“ГҮГ¶ then build the capability fully in-house so it fits our manifest schema, controls, and safety posture.

So the decision space for registry candidates should explicitly include:
1. onboard directly,
2. wrap temporarily, or
3. use as research/input for a native in-house skill.

comments.body:
Started the first substantive research pass for this issue.

Repo-local write-up created:
- `docs/hybrid-identity-research-pass-1-2026-04-01.md`

## First-pass findings

1. **Delegated identity already covers more human-style Teams behavior than bot-only does.**
   - Microsoft Graph supports delegated send, normal message update, soft delete, and reactions on real chat messages.
   - That means bot identity is not the full ceiling; delegated OBO remains a serious path for reactive, signed-in user-context collaboration.

2. **App-only is useful, but it is not full human-parity for Teams chat.**
   - Presence can be set app-only.
   - Online meetings can be created/get app-only with `OnlineMeetings.ReadWrite.All` plus application access policy.
   - Mail send, calendar access, and SharePoint/OneDrive access all have app-only stories.
   - But message reactions, soft delete, undo soft delete, and normal chat-message editing are not broadly mirrored by application permissions.

3. **The third identity tier is narrowing into a more specific justification.**
   - Right now the evidence suggests a persistent digital-worker identity is most justified for autonomous human-like collaboration / durable persona / mailbox-owner / OneDrive-owner / teammate semantics.
   - It is *not* yet justified merely by generic Microsoft 365 workload access, because delegated + app-only already cover a lot of that surface.

4. **The public Agent 365 ecosystem is now real, but still early.**
   - Confirmed official public repos:
     - `microsoft/Agent365-nodejs`
     - `microsoft/Agent365-Samples`
   - I do **not** yet see abundant public end-to-end examples of a mature, licensed human-style agent user behaving as a durable coworker across Teams/Outlook/OneDrive. The public code story is currently more SDK/tooling/sample-oriented than О“ГҮВЈfinished digital worker appО“ГҮВҘ oriented.

## Representative sources from this pass

- `https://learn.microsoft.com/graph/api/chatmessage-update?view=graph-rest-1.0`
- `https://learn.microsoft.com/graph/api/chatmessage-softdelete?view=graph-rest-1.0`
- `https://learn.microsoft.com/graph/api/chatmessage-setreaction?view=graph-rest-1.0`
- `https://learn.microsoft.com/graph/api/presence-setpresence?view=graph-rest-1.0`
- `https://learn.microsoft.com/graph/api/onlinemeeting-createorget?view=graph-rest-1.0`
- `https://learn.microsoft.com/graph/permissions-selected-overview#how-selected-scopes-work-with-sharepoint-and-onedrive-permissions`

## Open questions carried into pass 2

- What is the actual gap between О“ГҮВЈcan create/get a meeting objectО“ГҮВҘ and О“ГҮВЈcan join and behave as a true participantО“ГҮВҘ?
- Which of the observed bot limitations are eliminated by delegated identity versus only by a durable worker identity?
- How much of the autonomous workload story can sit on governed app-only access before we truly need a third identity tier?
- Is Agent ID / agent-user preview strategically important but still too preview-heavy for near-term HelkinSwarm implementation?

Next step: continue pass 2 around meeting participation, stronger agent-user/public-sample validation, and licensing/provisioning/governance costs.

Interim steering call after pass 1: **leave this investigation open**.

The current conclusion is **not** that HelkinSwarm does not need agentic personhood. The current conclusion is only that we do not yet have enough evidence to collapse onto a final identity architecture.

Why keep it open:

- delegated OBO already covers more human-style Teams behavior than bot-only does
- app-only still falls short of full human-like Teams chat parity
- and the third identity tier is most plausibly justified by **persistent personhood / autonomy requirements**, not by generic Microsoft 365 workload access alone

That means this issue becomes more important, not less, as the virtual-company design gets more concrete.

In particular, this research now looks directly relevant to:

- #237 О“ГҮГ¶ `[EPIC] Autonomous Virtual Company Self-Organization & Revenue Pipeline`
- #101 О“ГҮГ¶ `[EPIC] Virtual Employees & Nested Orchestrators (Post-MVP)`
- #102 О“ГҮГ¶ `Virtual employee factory - spawn nested instances`

The reason is simple: if the top-level stamp orchestrator or spawned virtual employees eventually need to act as persistent organizational actors О“ГҮГ¶ especially when communicating with outside humans, owning mailbox/OneDrive resources, or existing as durable teammates О“ГҮГ¶ then the agent-user / digital-worker identity question becomes a first-order architecture decision again.

I am treating #447 as an open research dependency for that future design space, not as a prematurely closed auth sidebar.

Repo-local docs updated to mirror this steering note:
- `docs/hybrid-identity-research-pass-1-2026-04-01.md`
- `docs/hybrid-identity-design-inflection-2026-04-01.md`

Second-pass research summary for #447. This is the evidence-backed recommendation, not a vibes-based one.

## Verified current HelkinSwarm state

I re-read the live repo/runtime files before writing this:

- `src/bot/HelkinSwarmBot.ts` О“ГҘГҶ `handleTeamsSigninTokenExchange()` bootstraps delegated OBO from the Teams token-exchange invoke.
- `src/functions/tabBootstrapObo.ts` О“ГҘГҶ `tab/bootstrap-obo` bootstraps delegated OBO from Teams tab SSO.
- `src/auth/oboTokenProvider.ts` О“ГҘГҶ `acquireTokenOnBehalfOf()` is the real delegated Graph path.
- `src/mcp/teamsTestHarness.ts` uses Graph device-code auth for local dev/test only.

I also re-checked `src/**` for a production worker-identity path (`agent user|agentic user|user_fic|Agent365|Agent 365|AgentIdentityBlueprint`) and found **no matches**.

So the current production architecture is still:

1. bot identity,
2. delegated OBO user context,
3. no live agent-user / persistent digital-worker runtime path in `src/` today.

## Second-pass Microsoft evidence

### 1. Microsoft now draws a hard line between interactive OBO agents and digital-worker identity

`Authenticate users and acquire tokens for interactive agents`:
- interactive agents use OBO
- and the page explicitly says that if the agent needs its **own user-like identity**, see **Agent users** / **Agent user OAuth flow** instead.

That is Microsoft telling us these are different architecture lanes, not one sliding scale.

### 2. Agent-user impersonation is real, but it is a separate and more complex protocol

`Agent's user account impersonation protocol` confirms:
- the protocol is `agent blueprint -> agent identity -> agent user`
- the flow uses chained credentials and `grant_type=user_fic`
- managed identity / FIC is the preferred production path
- client secrets are explicitly discouraged for production

So the modern answer is **not** О“ГҮВЈstore refresh tokens for a fake M365 user and hope for the best.О“ГҮВҘ

### 3. Agentic users really are user-like Microsoft 365 identities

`Agent 365 Identity` confirms agentic users:
- get `idtyp=user`
- can be licensed
- can have mailbox + OneDrive
- can appear in people cards / org charts
- can be @mentioned in Teams / docs / M365 apps
- are governed by a strict 1:1 parent-child relationship with the agent instance

This is the strongest first-party evidence that a true digital-worker identity track exists.

### 4. But Agent 365 is still preview / Frontier-gated

Both `Microsoft Agent 365 SDK Overview` and `Agent 365 Identity` are explicit that this is **Frontier preview** material.

That makes the direction strategically important, but it also means it is not a boring, low-risk near-term production dependency.

### 5. Agent-user operations also have real operational cost / latency / governance implications

`Agent 365 Identity` explicitly calls out:
- Microsoft 365 licenses are required for Teams/Email/Calendar/SharePoint/OneDrive access
- mailbox / OneDrive provisioning typically takes 10-15 minutes but can take up to 24 hours
- agentic users are governed through conditional access and explicit permission grants

This is a real operational design choice, not just a different token mint.

### 6. Grant-agent-access docs strengthen the case for a middle lane, not just a third lane

`Grant agents access to Microsoft 365 resources` explicitly says:
- delegated permissions are for interactive agents
- application permissions are for autonomous agents without a user present
- and access packages / RBAC / Exchange RBAC / Teams RSC can all be used to constrain access

That means the right long-term shape is not just:
- bot vs delegated vs agentic user

but also:
- **governed app-only workload access** as a first-class lane for autonomous mail/calendar/file work where Microsoft supports it.

## Public GitHub/code reference sweep

Concrete public references re-checked in this pass:

- `OfficeDev/Microsoft-Teams-Samples`
  - `samples/TeamsJS/app-sso/nodejs/server/bots/botActivityHandler.js`
  - `samples/graph-file-fetch/nodejs/ssoOauthHelper.js`
- `microsoftgraph/msgraph-sdk-javascript`
  - `samples/javascript/SampleRequests.js`

What they show:
- Teams SSO/tokenExchange patterns are public, normal, and dedup-sensitive.
- The `ssoOauthHelper.js` sample explicitly deduplicates repeated `signin/tokenExchange` invokes across Teams clients and returns `412 PreconditionFailed` when exchange fails.
- Graph JS samples cover `/users/me/sendMail`, `/me/events`, and `/me/drive/.../content` style user-context operations.

That strengthens the current HelkinSwarm direction for delegated OBO and user-context Graph work.

## Recommendation matrix

### Recommended now

Adopt and formalize this target posture:

1. **Keep Bot identity** for install/bootstrap/command surface/notifications.
2. **Keep delegated OBO** for reactive user-context actions.
3. **Add / formalize a governed app-only workload lane** where Microsoft already supports autonomous access (mail/calendar/files/presence/meeting-object operations under proper policy constraints).

This is the lowest-regret next-step architecture.

### Possible but risky

A classic licensed M365 user-account pattern remains possible, but I do **not** recommend it as the preferred target architecture right now.

Why:
- it invites old refresh-token-hoarding instincts,
- complicates MFA / conditional access / lifecycle,
- and Microsoft is now offering a more explicit agent-user model instead.

### Preview-only / strategic watch item

**Entra Agent ID + agentic user / Agent 365** should be treated as the strategic future path for durable digital-worker personhood, but **preview-only for now**.

Use this lane when one or more of these becomes a real requirement:
- persistent mailbox-owning / OneDrive-owning virtual employees
- durable teammate semantics in Teams/M365
- independent organizational personhood for HelkinSwarm or spawned virtual workers
- human-like presence/mentionability/resource ownership that delegated or app-only cannot express cleanly

### Not worth pursuing

Do **not** treat these as the target path:
- ROPC
- client-secret-centric О“ГҮВЈfake humanО“ГҮВҘ automation as the intended end state
- stored refresh-token workarounds as the preferred architecture

MicrosoftО“ГҮГ–s current agent docs point in a better direction, and the security posture is cleaner.

## Final recommendation for #447

My evidence-backed recommendation is:

- **Near-term production target:** Bot + Delegated OBO + Governed App-Only Workload Lane
- **Deferred strategic path:** Agent-user / Agent 365 identity for future durable digital-worker semantics
- **Explicitly defer** classic licensed-user impersonation as the preferred architecture unless preview constraints force a temporary bridge later

## Proposed follow-on backlog shape

Instead of keeping this issue open forever as a research tarpit, I recommend:

1. Use `#443` as the parent design issue / future epic shell.
2. Add a follow-on planning/design issue for the **governed app-only workload lane** if it does not already exist.
3. Keep agent-user / Agent 365 work in the **preview-watch / virtual-employee strategy lane** until the virtual-company / virtual-employee requirements become concrete enough to justify it.
4. Re-cast `#443` as an `[EPIC]` only when that child split is ready to be explicit.

## Exit decision

I consider the research objective of `#447` satisfied:
- recommendation produced
- supported-now vs risky vs preview-only vs not-worth-pursuing distinguished
- parent issue guidance updated next
- follow-on direction proposed from evidence rather than speculation

title: [RESEARCH] Hybrid identity for HelkinSwarm: bot bootstrap + delegated OBO + digital-worker identity track

comments.body:
Closed by commit 246c9ee (`fix(#445): make teams package workflow build only`).

## Root cause correction
This turned out **not** to be a simple missing-permission bug on the CICD principal.

Repo and Microsoft documentation evidence now agree on the deeper problem:
- the workflow was attempting Teams app-catalog publish from a GitHub OIDC / app-only context
- Microsoft Graph Teams app publish/update APIs are **delegated-only** for work/school accounts
- official docs for both publish and update state: **Application permissions: Not supported**

So the old workflow was built around an unsupported automation model, not just a missing app-role grant.

## Repo evidence gathered
### Tenant/app state
Verified the known CICD app directly:
- client id: `97e5a7b7-9cd7-4019-9bc3-f9498f18ca6e`
- display name: `HelkinSwarm-v2-CICD`
- current API permissions list: `[]`

That explained the earlier `403`, but the Microsoft docs showed that even adding application permissions would not make this publish path valid.

### Microsoft docs grounding
Official Microsoft Graph docs used during this fix:
- `Publish teamsApp`
- `Update teamsApp`

Both document the same permission model:
- Delegated (work or school): `AppCatalog.Submit` or `AppCatalog.ReadWrite.All`
- **Application: Not supported**

## What changed
Updated `.github/workflows/teams-package.yml` so it is now honest and build-only:
- removed the Azure OIDC / Graph publish path
- removed the implication that CI can publish to Teams app catalog
- summary now explicitly states that Graph publish in CI is not supported with application permissions
- summary points to delegated/manual fallback:
  - `./appPackage/New-TeamsAppPackage.ps1 -Publish`

## Validation
Shipped commit:
- `246c9ee` О“ГҮГ¶ `fix(#445): make teams package workflow build only`

Validation run:
- workflow: `Teams App Package`
- run: `23835034552`
- result: О“ВЈГ  success

Observed from the run log:
- artifact `HelkinSwarm-1.0.20` built and uploaded successfully
- workflow emitted:
  - `publish-status=delegated-required`
  - `Teams app catalog publish remains delegated/manual.`
  - `Microsoft Graph teamsApp publish/update APIs do not support application permissions`
  - `Publish was requested, but this workflow intentionally stayed build-only.`

## Honest resolution
So the issue is fixed by correcting the workflow contract:
- CI now does the part it can honestly do (build/package artifact)
- delegated/manual publish remains the supported path
- the false expectation that OIDC app-only CI could publish to the Teams app catalog is removed

title: [BUG] Teams app package workflow cannot publish to app catalog because CICD principal lacks Graph app-catalog permissions
Design inflection: bot vs user identity for Teams AI copilots

comments.body:
Real-world screenshot evidence from the current live bot account confirms the practical bot-identity ceiling this issue is about.

## Attached screenshot evidence (2026-04-01)
The current Teams DM shows **both easter eggs failing in bot-specific ways** rather than behaving like a real user account would:

### 1. High-five path still degrades to literal shortcode text
Observed in the screenshot:
- user sends the high-five-style emoji interaction
- bot reply at `06:33` is the literal text:
  - `(highfive)`

So the bot is **not** producing a native Teams Together Emoji / high-five effect. It is falling back to raw shortcode text.

### 2. RobotLove path degrades to static preview + file-consent upload flow
Observed in the screenshot:
- the heart-eyes-robot interaction does not arrive back as a native animated inline emoji/GIF experience
- instead the bot posts:
  - a static image preview
  - helper text: `Teams bot image previews are static, so tap below if you want the full animated RobotLove.gif.`
  - a file-consent card asking permission to upload `RobotLove.gif` to OneDrive

So the bot cannot simply behave like a human teammate posting an inline animated reaction/GIF in chat. It has to fall back to a **preview + file handoff** pattern.

## Why this matters architecturally
This is not just a cute-easter-egg annoyance.

It is concrete evidence that the current **bot account / bot identity architecture** is materially different from a real Teams/M365 user identity in exactly the ways that would matter for:
- richer conversational UX
- persistent AI coworker / virtual employee personas
- meeting participation / roster presence
- reactions / edits / human-like chat affordances
- future "AI teammate" or agent-account designs

In other words: the screenshot is a real-world reference point showing that the current bot account is hitting platform limits, not merely implementation bugs in one narrow code path.

## Design consequence to investigate
This strengthens the case that HelkinSwarm may eventually need one of these models instead of a pure bot-identity architecture:
- delegated user identity for human-parity actions
- dedicated licensed AI user accounts / service users
- hybrid architecture:
  - bot for installation/commands/notifications
  - user identity or AI employee account for human-parity Teams behavior

That decision could affect not only the current orchestrator, but also the future virtual-employees architecture, where persistent presence and human-like interaction may matter a lot more than classic bot-command semantics.

Created the first linked child issue for this design fork:

- #447 О“ГҮГ¶ [RESEARCH] Hybrid identity for HelkinSwarm: bot bootstrap + delegated OBO + digital-worker identity track

Why this one first:

- We already have verified OBO/delegated wiring in the current runtime (`HelkinSwarmBot.handleTeamsSigninTokenExchange()`, `tab/bootstrap-obo`, `acquireTokenOnBehalfOf()`), so the next question is not whether delegated identity exists.
- The unresolved question is whether HelkinSwarm should stay bot + delegated only, or add a third identity tier for a persistent digital worker / agent user / licensed M365 account.
- I do not currently see that third identity tier wired in production runtime code; only the local test harness uses device-code auth.

This child issue is explicitly scoped to:

- deeper Microsoft Docs research,
- GitHub code/reference search,
- capability + constraints matrix,
- recommendation of the target hybrid identity path.

Intentionally deferred until the research lands:

- the design-decision child issue,
- planning / execution child issues,
- re-casting `#443` as an `[EPIC]`.

IО“ГҮГ–d rather earn the epic label with evidence than staple it on with vibes.

Started substantive research work under child issue #447 and mirrored it into the repo docs.

New repo-local note:
- `docs/hybrid-identity-research-pass-1-2026-04-01.md`

Parent-level delta from pass 1:

- delegated identity already appears to cover more human-style Teams message behavior than a pure bot identity does (send/edit/delete/react on real message objects)
- app-only is still useful for presence, some meeting flows, mail/calendar, and file workloads, but it does **not** look like a full replacement for human-like Teams chat behavior
- this is narrowing the justification for a third identity tier toward persistent personhood / autonomous teammate semantics rather than generic Microsoft 365 access

Details and source links are in #447; I am continuing from there rather than spraying half-finished conclusions across the parent.

Parent-level delta from completed research child `#447`.

## Recommendation

The current evidence supports this target posture for HelkinSwarm:

1. **Keep Bot identity** for install/bootstrap/notifications/command surface.
2. **Keep delegated OBO** for reactive signed-in user-context work.
3. **Formalize a governed app-only workload lane** for autonomous mail/calendar/file/presence/meeting-object operations where Microsoft supports it.
4. Treat **Agent user / Agent 365** as the strategic future path for durable digital-worker personhood, but **preview-only for now**.

## Why

### Repo-grounded current state

Verified again in the live repo:
- `src/bot/HelkinSwarmBot.ts` О“ГҘГҶ `handleTeamsSigninTokenExchange()`
- `src/functions/tabBootstrapObo.ts` О“ГҘГҶ `tab/bootstrap-obo`
- `src/auth/oboTokenProvider.ts` О“ГҘГҶ `acquireTokenOnBehalfOf()`

These are the real delegated identity seams already wired today.

I do **not** see a production agent-user / worker-identity runtime path in `src/` today.

### First-party Microsoft direction

The docs are now explicit that:
- interactive agents use OBO,
- digital-worker scenarios use **Agent users / agent-user OAuth flow**,
- agentic users can have `idtyp=user`, mailbox, OneDrive, licenses, @mentionability,
- but Agent 365 remains **Frontier preview**,
- and agent-user provisioning/licensing adds real operational cost and latency.

### Public-code references

Concrete public implementation breadcrumbs remain strongest on the delegated path:
- `OfficeDev/Microsoft-Teams-Samples` tokenExchange / SSO samples
- `microsoftgraph/msgraph-sdk-javascript` mail/calendar/drive samples

## Practical conclusion

The next safe architecture step is **not** О“ГҮВЈjump straight to a fake licensed userО“ГҮВҘ and it is **not** О“ГҮВЈpretend bot + app-only already equals human parity.О“ГҮВҘ

The safer near-term target is:
- bot bootstrap,
- delegated OBO for user-context collaboration,
- app-only where Microsoft explicitly supports autonomous workload access,
- agent-user kept as the future durable-personhood lane when virtual employees / virtual-company semantics actually require it.

I am treating `#447` as complete research and closing it on that basis rather than leaving it open as endless identity philosophy.

Shipped a concrete infra fix for this fresh-runtime no-reply repro in commit `53fb15e` (`fix(#410): keep router warm in low cost mode`).

## Root cause found
Repo + live Azure evidence now points to the **global router** as the missing front-door piece, not the stamp handler:
- `src/router/routerFunction.ts` is the actual Teams ingress front door when the global router bot is in use
- `appPackage/manifest.json` points the Teams app at router bot id `42d3359f-8757-421d-a853-fb2960cf2dac`
- `infra/main-router.bicep` previously allowed `routerMinReplicas = 0` in low-cost mode
- live Azure verification before the fix showed:
  - router app `helkinswarm-router`
  - `minReplicas = 0`
  - router bot endpoint = `https://helkinswarm-router.salmonbeach-d4db91b5.eastus2.azurecontainerapps.io/api/messages`

That exactly matches the failure shape seen here: the stamp showed no `/api/messages` request because the request could vanish at the router before the stamp ever observed it.

## What changed
- `infra/main-router.bicep`
  - router now stays warm even in low-cost mode (`routerMinReplicas = 1`)
  - comments/documentation now explicitly state the global Teams ingress front door must not scale to zero
- `infra/main-router.json`
  - kept in sync with the Bicep change
- `.github/workflows/deploy-router.yml`
- `docs/12-Deployment-CICD.md`
- `docs/03-Tech-Stack-Infrastructure.md`

## Validation
### Local
- `az bicep build --file infra/main-router.bicep` О“ВЈГ 
- `pnpm build` О“ВЈГ 

### Deploy
- push commit: `53fb15e`
- push-triggered stamp workflow: `Deploy Stamp` run `23832199854` О“ВЈГ 
- dispatched router workflow: `Deploy Router` run `23832207733` О“ВЈГ 

### Live Azure state after deploy
- router revision: `helkinswarm-router--r53fb15ed`
- router `minReplicas = 1` О“ВЈГ 
- router bot endpoint still points at the router `/api/messages` О“ВЈГ 

### Conversational E2E
Fresh-runtime first probe after startup notice:
- sent `/status`
- message id: `1775019015159`
- correlation: `MCP-19D4760C679`
- result: О“ВЈГ  reply in `3.3s`
- bot reply: `HelkinSwarm 1.0.8 О“ГҮГ¶ maintenance: OFF, safety: confirmation-gated, tools: 32`

Critical before/after health evidence:
- `runtimeBefore`: `messagePath = degraded`, `lastSuccessAt = null`
- `runtimeAfter`: `messagePath = ok`, `lastSuccessAt = 2026-04-01T04:50:16.581Z`

That is the exact fresh-runtime first-turn shape this issue captured, and it no longer vanished.

Additional lane checks:
- `/light ... ROUTER410-LIGHT` completed successfully with final reply `ROUTER410-LIGHT`
- `/heavy ... ROUTER410-HEAVY` also produced a final bot response (the wording was too GitHub-like and it chose to act on issue `#410`, but the turn was delivered and completed rather than disappearing)

Given the shipped fix + successful fresh-runtime first-turn validation, IО“ГҮГ–m treating this specific repro issue as fixed.

Then tell me plainly whether the email was actually sent.` Observed final bot reply: > `The email was sent successfully.` Telemetry footer: - `E2E:14056ms` - model `o4m` - cost `вүЎЖ’В¬Г–$0.0270` ### Secondary lane probe Message sent: > `/light Use the exact tool outlook_send_email to send an HTML email to eric@eanderson.de with subject "DL-20260401-secondary-html-send" ... Then tell me plainly whether the email was actually sent.` Observed final bot reply: > `Yes, Mr. Anderson. The email was actually sent.` Telemetry footer: - `E2E:9095ms` - model `gpt-5.4-mini-2026-03-17` - cost `вүЎЖ’ГҶв••$0.0189` ## Important remaining live gap I also probed the new inline-runtime-asset contract with a deliberately nonexistent asset id to verify the deployed failure mode: > `/heavy ... body "

Inline contract probe

" ... inlineAssets [{"assetId":"11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111","contentId":"hero-image"}] ...` Observed final bot reply: ```text helkin_skill_search: (result too large to display inline О“ГҮГ¶ 4284 chars) outlook_send_email failed: Runtime asset '11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111' is not available anymore. Please upload or re-materialize it again before sending the email. ``` That means the handler-side error is honest, but the deployed user-facing summarization path still leaks raw tool sludge for this failure shape. I filed that as: - `#454` О“ГҮГ¶ `[BUG] Missing runtime asset in inline-email probe still leaks raw tool sludge to the user` ## Current status I am **not closing `#439` yet** from this validation pass. Reason: - the underlying runtime-asset attachment/embed implementation is now shipped and locally well-covered - basic live `outlook_send_email` sends work on deployed lanes - but the deployed UX still has a relevant live failure-path gap (`#454`) - and the Teams conversational harness still does not give me a clean real-upload О“ГҘГҶ runtime-asset О“ГҘГҶ inline-email happy-path E2E proof in one conversational probe So this issue has materially advanced, but it is not yet honest to mark it fully done from live validation evidence alone.

Delta update: the live failure-path bug filed as `#454` is now fixed and closed.

- Commit: `5e8a763`
- Deploy run: `23849380643`

## Revalidation result

Re-ran the same missing-runtime-asset inline-email probe after deploy:

> `/heavy Use the exact tool outlook_send_email ... inlineAssets [{"assetId":"11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111","contentId":"hero-image"}] ...`

So the deployed UX no longer leaks raw `helkin_skill_search` / raw tool failure sludge for that inline-runtime-asset failure shape.

## Current state of this issue

I am still leaving `#439` open.

Reason: I still do **not** have an honest deployed Teams conversational proof of the full happy path:

- real uploaded or otherwise materialized runtime asset
- passed through the live conversation/tool pipeline
- sent as an Outlook email with working inline render

The current Teams harness is good enough to prove:

but not yet a true upload/materialize О“ГҘГҶ inline-send success path in one live conversational E2E.

Delta update from fresh deployed probing + attached screenshot evidence.

## New live regression evidence

### 1) Raw tool sludge is back on the inline-email path
Attached screenshot + matching live transcript show this deployed reply:

```text
helkin_skill_search: (result too large to display inline О“ГҮГ¶ 4944 chars)

outlook_send_email failed: Inline runtime-asset email composition could not be completed. The HTML body references cid:gif-inline", but matching inline runtime assets were not supplied. I have not sent the requested email.
```

Telemetry footer from the same reply:
- `E2E:17389ms`
- model `gpt-5.4-mini-2026-03-17`
- cost `$0.0201`

This is a regression relative to the earlier `#454` fix because raw tool sludge is again reaching the final user-facing reply for an inline-email failure shape.

### 2) The live path is still generating bad cid/runtime-asset mappings
The same failure text shows a mismatched content-id path:
- body referenced `cid:gif-inline`
- but the runtime assets supplied to the tool did not match that content id

So the system is still capable of constructing an inline-email send attempt whose HTML/body cid map does not line up with the runtime asset contract.

### 3) Fresh duplicate-send symptom reported by the user
New user report from the same validation cycle:

> every time an email send is triggered i get two identical emails in my private mailbox

I have not yet root-caused that in code, but it is serious and likely points to either:
- duplicated `outlook_send_email` execution,
- duplicate send trigger after a follow-up/tool retry path,
- or another orchestration replay/delivery edge around mail-send actions.

## Current state
This issue remains open.

I am now treating the duplicate-email symptom as part of the same active investigation until the code says otherwise. If it turns out to be a distinct root cause from the inline-runtime-asset mapping/regression, I will split it into a dedicated bug with receipts rather than muddy this issue.

Final delta update from the duplicate-send investigation.

## Duplicate-email bug: fixed

### Root cause progression
The earlier planner/follow-up duplicate suppression was not sufficient on its own. The bug survived because semantically equivalent `outlook_send_email` requests could still reach the actual Graph send boundary under replay/retry pressure.

The durable fix was to move the strongest idempotency guard to the real side-effect boundary:
- `skills/outlook/handlers.ts`
- before `POST /me/sendMail`
- backed by the shared outbound-artifact claim store in `src/bot/conversationStore.ts`

### Shipping sequence
- `e775002` О“ГҮГ¶ same-turn mutating-call suppression in orchestrator/dispatch
- `fb87091` О“ГҮГ¶ semantic normalization for duplicate email fingerprints
- `2719d9b` О“ГҮГ¶ handler-level Outlook send idempotency at the actual mail-send boundary
- `64dfdfd` О“ГҮГ¶ clarified duplicate-suppression wording so the bot does not falsely imply the original send failed
- `7eb1a09` О“ГҮГ¶ documented the reusable outbound idempotency primitive in instructions + docs

## Live validation evidence

### Earlier failing secondary-lane behavior (before handler-level idempotency)
- subject `DL-dupcheck-secondary-e775002`
- sent-items verification reply: `Exact subject appears twice.`

### Final passing secondary-lane behavior (after `2719d9b` + `64dfdfd`)
Human send probe:
> `/light Use the exact tool outlook_send_email ... subject "DL-dupcheck-secondary-64dfdfd" ...`

Final bot reply:
> `Yes, Mr. Anderson О“ГҮГ¶ the email was actually sent.`

Telemetry footer:
- `E2E:8124ms`
- model `gpt-5.4-mini-2026-03-17`
- cost `$0.0188`

Mailbox verification probe for the same exact subject:
> inspect sentitems and count exact subject `DL-dupcheck-secondary-64dfdfd`

Final bot reply:
> `Exactly one email matches.`

That is the live proof I needed: one Teams request, one mailbox send.

## Documentation shipped
I also documented the now-proven reusable primitive for future skills/orchestrator work:
- `.github/instructions/idempotency.instructions.md`
- `.github/instructions/orchestrator-patterns.instructions.md`
- `.github/instructions/skills-library.instructions.md`
- `docs/0t-Idempotency-and-External-Side-Effects.md`
- `docs/08-Orchestrator-Patterns.md`
- `docs/10-Teams-Interface.md`

The canonical rule is now explicit: planner-level dedup is helpful, but the strongest guard must live at the activity/handler that actually emits the external side-effect.

## Current issue status
I am **not** closing `#439` from this comment alone.

What is now honestly proven:
- duplicate Outlook sends from one conversational turn were happening
- the duplicate-send bug is now fixed on live validation
- the repo now has a reusable documented idempotency primitive for future user-visible side-effects

What I still do not claim from this thread:
- a full live conversational happy-path proof for real Teams-uploaded inline media rendering correctly inside Outlook mail body under the original screenshot scenario

Validation update from a fresh live pass on the deployed bot.

I did **not** get the remaining happy-path proof for this issue today, and the reason is now clearer: the active deployed lanes are still drifting Outlook read/search requests into discovery metadata instead of actually executing mailbox tools.

## What I tested live

### Primary lane (`/light`)
Prompt:

### Secondary lane (`/heavy`)

- `/heavy Search my Outlook inbox for emails with attachments. Return only the top 5 message ids, subjects, and receivedAt values as compact JSON.`

Observed final reply:
- discovery/search metadata listing Outlook capability groups/tools
- **not** actual mailbox results

Telemetry:
- `E2E:8026ms`
- model `o4m`

## Why this matters for `#439`

The remaining honest close criterion here was live E2E proof of:
- locate/materialize a real runtime asset
- embed it inline in a sent Outlook email
- verify the happy path on deployed behavior

That flow is currently blocked because the deployed bot is not reliably executing the Outlook read/search steps needed to obtain a real attachment-backed runtime asset through conversation.

## Repo-grounded context read before this pass

I re-read the live-wired implementation files:
- `skills/outlook/handlers.ts`
- `skills/outlook/manifest.json`
- `src/integrations/runtimeAssetStore.ts`
- `src/bot/inboundAttachmentIngestion.ts`
- `src/orchestrator/discoveryToolInjection.ts`

What I verified in code:
- the Outlook handlers for `outlook_search_emails`, `outlook_list_attachments`, and `outlook_download_attachment` do exist
- the remaining blocker today is deployed routing/execution behavior, not me forgetting to read the files

## Follow-up

I am filing a dedicated bug for the deployed routing drift because it is broader than this one issue and needs its own receipts.

So `#439` remains open, now blocked by that separate live Outlook read/search execution gap.

Shipped a real fix sequence for this bug and validated it live. ## What changed Three commits addressed different parts of the failure family: - `d2642e6` О“ГҮГ¶ `fix(#438): block broken inline email fallback` - `skills/outlook/handlers.ts` - `outlook_send_email` now rejects unsupported embedded inline-image HTML (`cid:` / `data:image`) instead of sending a broken email body - `skills/outlook/manifest.json` - tool description now explicitly says embedded inline images / file attachments are not supported yet - `src/orchestrator/llmFollowUpActivity.ts` - empty follow-up fallback no longer dumps raw discovery/tool blobs for this request shape; it now produces an honest blocker message - `86a5659` О“ГҮГ¶ `fix(#438): stop repeating blocked inline email send` - first loop-brake attempt for repeated doomed retries - `22fb319` О“ГҮГ¶ `fix(#438): stop retries after blocked tool class` - broadened the retry brake so mutated argument variants of the same deterministically blocked tool no longer keep getting reissued ## Local validation Ran repeatedly during the fix sequence: - `pnpm test -- tests/orchestrator/llmFollowUpPrompt.test.ts tests/skills/outlookSendEmail.test.ts tests/skills/outlookManifest.test.ts tests/skills/outlookAttachments.test.ts` - `pnpm build` Final result on the last pass: - О“ВЈГ  4/4 focused test files passed - О“ВЈГ  11/11 tests passed - О“ВЈГ  build passed ## Deploy evidence Successful deploys for the fix sequence: - `23799632757` for `d2642e6` - `23800421999` for `86a5659` - `23801210679` for `22fb319` ## Live outside-in validation ### Final probe on the latest build Probe sent in Teams after the final deploy: - `Use the exact tool outlook_send_email to send an HTML email ... ... Then tell me plainly whether the email was actually sent or blocked.` Correlation: - `30c6c3ba` Observed final bot reply: - `I couldnО“ГҮГ–t complete that email request. Outlook inline embedded images from Teams/runtime assets are not supported yet, so I did not send the requested inline-image email.` Telemetry footer on that final reply: - `tools:helkin_skill_search,outlook_send_email` - cost dropped to `вүЎЖ’ГҶв•Ў$0.0363` That is materially better than the original failure, which produced: - raw nonsense / tool dump output - repeated low-value tool churn - a misleading/broken send path - much higher cost (`$0.0882` in the original screenshot) ### Important live behavior change The final validated probe no longer showed the repeated `outlook_send_email` spam loop. The correlated final reply only reflected: - `helkin_skill_search` - one `outlook_send_email` attempt So the current live behavior is now: - honest blocker message О“ВЈГ  - no fake success claim О“ВЈГ  - no broken inline-image send О“ВЈГ  - no repeated blocked tool-class retry spiral О“ВЈГ  ## Conclusion This bug is resolved as a **bug**: - the system no longer returns raw garbage for this request shape - it no longer attempts to send a knowingly broken inline-image email body - it now tells the user plainly that the requested inline-image email was **not** sent - the repeated blocked-send retry pattern is cut down to a single failed attempt ## Remaining product gap What still does **not** exist is the feature itself: - Outlook inline-image email composition from Teams/runtime assets That is a separate capability gap, not the same bug anymore. I am capturing that as a dedicated follow-on issue so this bug can close without losing the remaining work.

title: [BUG] Image-to-inline-email request devolved into repeated tool churn and nonsense response instead of drafting the requested email

comments.body:
Progress captured here so the attachment-validation audit can be resumed cleanly later without losing the thread.

## Immediate fix completed

### Manifest bump shipped
- commit: `1a6bd1e`
- message: `fix(#437): bump teams app package to 1.0.20`

`appPackage/manifest.json` is now:
- `"version": "1.0.20"`

and the tab URLs in the same manifest were kept aligned to `v=1.0.20` for internal consistency.

### Fresh Teams app package artifact produced
Because `.github/workflows/teams-package.yml` is **manual only**, I explicitly ran it after the push.

Successful package run:
- workflow: `Teams App Package`
- run id: `23798017183`
- commit: `1a6bd1e`
- result: О“ВЈГ  success

Artifact produced:
- artifact name: `HelkinSwarm-1.0.20`
- artifact size: `58981` bytes

So there is now a fresh installable package artifact for the file-support manifest revision.

## Why the broader audit is deferred into this issue

The side-job goal was to unblock installation quickly and preserve the research/work queue without derailing the SkillForge work in flight.

This issue is therefore the parking place for the deeper follow-up audit rather than trying to reopen half the backlog mid-stream.

## Research summary to preserve

### Confirmed root cause
- `supportsFiles: true` had already been committed in `appPackage/manifest.json`
- but the manifest version remained at `1.0.19`
- the Teams package workflow does **not** auto-run on push
- the Teams package workflow does **not** auto-bump versions

So there was a real gap between:
- repo manifest state
- installable Teams package state

### Most likely impacted validation area
The issues most worth revisiting after the `1.0.20` package is installed are:
- `#415` О“ГҮГ¶ proof-path/validation meta issue
- `#416` О“ГҮГ¶ inbound Teams attachment ingestion
- `#413` О“ГҮГ¶ epic closure confidence, but only if the corrected validation changes the child confidence materially

Secondary spot-check only:
- `#419` О“ГҮГ¶ outbound Teams file/image reply support
  - less directly blocked by inbound upload capability, but worth sanity-checking after the new package is installed

## Work todo when this gets picked back up

- [ ] Confirm the `1.0.20` Teams app package is actually installed/sideloaded in the active client
- [ ] Run a **real user file upload in Teams chat** against the new package
- [ ] Revisit `#415` and decide whether its upload-driven validation note needs amendment
- [ ] Revisit `#416` and confirm whether real inbound upload validation now passes with the corrected app package
- [ ] Revisit `#413` only if the corrected validation materially changes epic-level closure confidence
- [ ] Optionally spot-check `#419` with the new package installed

## Honesty note
This preserved issue is about the **validation gap**, not an automatic claim that the runtime attachment implementation was fake.

The missing package version bump created a legitimate reason to distrust the completeness of the **real upload-path validation**, so that audit needs to be resumed explicitly from here.

Progress update on #437: the manifest-version part is now real, and a fresh package artifact has been built.

Confirmed current repo state:
- appPackage/manifest.json now has version 1.0.20
- bots[0].supportsFiles is true

Package artifact evidence:
- workflow: Teams App Package
- run: 23815874519
- result: success
- package/build steps passed
- artifact upload step passed
- publish was intentionally skipped (build-only mode)

What this means:
- the specific bug described in #437 (supportsFiles changed without any manifest version bump) is no longer true in the repo
- there is now a fresh build artifact tied to the bumped manifest version

What is still not done:
- install/upload the new package revision in Teams Admin Center
- re-run a real user file-upload validation in chat
- revisit #415, #416, #413 (and optionally #419) with evidence from the corrected package revision

So I am keeping #437 open for the remaining validation/install steps, but step 1 and step 2 are now materially advanced.

Closing this as fixed at the repo/package level.

## What is now true
- `appPackage/manifest.json` is on `1.0.20`
- `bots[0].supportsFiles = true`
- fresh package artifacts for `1.0.20` exist
  - earlier build-only run: `23815874519`
  - latest publish-attempt run: `23834396798`

So the specific bug described here О“ГҮГ¶ **manifest capability changed without a package version bump / installable package revision** О“ГҮГ¶ is no longer true.

## What I re-checked today
I also attempted the missing publish step via:
- workflow: `Teams App Package`
- run: `23834396798`
- inputs: publish enabled (`skip_publish=false`)

Result:
- artifact build still succeeded О“ВЈГ 
- publish step failed with `403 Forbidden` from Graph on both POST and PUT app-catalog endpoints

That is a **different blocker** than the original version-bump bug.

## Follow-up split out
I created a separate issue for the remaining publish blocker:
- **Teams app package workflow cannot publish to app catalog because CICD principal lacks Graph app-catalog permissions**

That new issue now tracks the Graph permission / app-catalog publication problem.

## Honest scope note
This issue never proved the real user file-upload path by itself; it identified the repo/package-version gap that could invalidate that validation story.

That repo-side gap is now fixed.
Real upload-path validation still belongs with the downstream attachment issues (`#415`, `#416`, and only indirectly `#413`).

Shipped a concrete fix candidate in commit `c560b87` (`fix(#433): prefer explicit discovery tool intents`) but I do **not** have a clean live pass/fail verdict yet.

## Repo-grounded fix
- `src/orchestrator/discoveryToolInjection.ts`
  - `getForcedDiscoveryFollowUpToolChoice()` now honors explicit tool-name mentions before the generic `send|email|mail => outlook_send_email` fallback
  - added read/search/attachment intent preference for `outlook_search_emails`, `outlook_read_email`, `outlook_list_attachments`, and `outlook_download_attachment`
- `tests/orchestrator/discoveryToolInjection.test.ts`
  - added regression coverage for the exact live failure shape:
    - explicit `outlook_search_emails` mention beats `outlook_send_email`
    - generic mailbox search intent chooses `outlook_search_emails`

## Local validation
Ran:

## Deploy
- commit: `c560b87`
- workflow: `Deploy Stamp`
- run: `23785045576`
- result: О“ВЈГ  success

## Live revalidation attempt
I attempted to rerun the same exact-tool Outlook search prompt after the deploy:
- `/light Use the exact tool outlook_search_emails with query "hasAttachment:true" and return only the first result as JSON with fields id, subject, from, hasAttachments.`
- human message id: `1774941448650`
- correlation observed in chat: `a446e286-e10c-4335-8c84-17fddc931736`

What happened:
- the turn no longer failed fast with the old `outlook_search_emails is not available in this tool surface` text
- but the runtime restarted mid-validation (`Runtime Online` / `shutting down` notices appeared during the same window)
- the exact probe therefore became inconclusive as evidence for the fix itself

Current honest status:
- local fix is real and shipped
- the previous exact error did **not** immediately reappear on the first post-fix probe
- but I do **not** yet have a stable post-deploy conversational result that proves the tool-surface bug is resolved end-to-end

Leaving this open pending a clean rerun on a stable runtime window.

Delivered in two shipped commits:
- `c560b87` О“ГҮГ¶ `fix(#433): prefer explicit discovery tool intents`
- `6f3743b` О“ГҮГ¶ `fix(#433): force discovery for exact tool prompts`

## Repo-grounded fix
- `src/orchestrator/discoveryToolInjection.ts`
  - `getForcedDiscoveryFollowUpToolChoice()` now prefers:

    - before falling back to the generic `send|email|mail => outlook_send_email` rule
  - `shouldForceDiscoveryToolSearch()` now forces discovery when the prompt contains:
    - explicit tool-like names such as `outlook_list_attachments`
    - attachment-specific cues such as `attachment`, `cid`, `content id`, `messageId`
- `tests/orchestrator/discoveryToolInjection.test.ts`

## Local validation
Ran:

Result:

- О“ВЈГ  24/24 tests passed
- О“ВЈГ  build passed

## Deploys
- `c560b87` О“ГҘГҶ workflow `23785045576` О“ГҘГҶ О“ВЈГ  success
- `6f3743b` О“ГҘГҶ workflow `23785625821` О“ГҘГҶ О“ВЈГ  success

## Fresh live validation
The exact failure repro from the issue body was rerun after the fix.

### Secondary lane
Probe:

Fresh live retest on the deployed runtime after commit `7025784` (`fix(#431): dedup quoted reply retries by activity id`) no longer reproduces this regression.

## Deploy context
- commit: `7025784`
- workflow: `Deploy Stamp`
- run: `23782680329`
- result: О“ВЈГ  success

## Primary-lane retest (`/heavy`)
Sent at `2026-03-31T06:14:46.363Z`:

`/heavy please put a new meeting in my calendar to have lunch with a friend tomorrow at 12:30 with a reminder 15 minutes before. This is issue 432 retest after 7025784 primary 0331G.`

Message id:
- `1774937686363`

Observed:
- О“ВЈГ  immediate visible bot ack:
  - `О“Г®Вў Working on it... (вүЎЖ’Г¶Г‘ frontier model)`
  - bot message id `1774937687917`
- О“ВЈГ  later visible confirmation text:
  - `О“Г…в••вҲ©в••Г… Awaiting your approval above before I continue with outlook_create_calendar_event.`

- confirmation card payload correlation:

Runtime after completion of the visible handoff:
- `status = healthy`
- `messagePath = ok`
- `pendingAcks = 0`
- active turn moved to honest `awaiting-confirmation`

This is the exact behavior the issue previously failed to reach.

## Secondary-lane control (`/light`)
Sent at `2026-03-31T06:16:43.300Z`:

`/light please put a new meeting in my calendar to have lunch with a friend tomorrow at 12:30 with a reminder 15 minutes before. This is issue 432 retest after 7025784 secondary 0331H.`

Message id:
- `1774937803300`

Observed:
- О“ВЈГ  immediate visible bot ack:
  - `О“Г®Вў Working on it... (О“ГңГӯ fast model)`
  - bot message id `1774937803612`

  - `О“Г…в••вҲ©в••Г… Awaiting your approval above before I continue with outlook_create_calendar_event.`

## Interpretation
This regression is no longer present on the shipped runtime.

The most likely effective fix is the `7025784` handoff/dedup work that:
- checks for duplicate overseer instances before sending the placeholder
- keys durable dedup to the exact Teams `activity.id` when available
- suppresses duplicate placeholders rather than leaving an orphaned visible turn

Closing as fixed by current deployed behavior.

comments.body:
Delivered in commit `7025784` (`fix(#431): dedup quoted reply retries by activity id`).

## What changed
- `src/bot/overseerDedupIdentity.ts`
  - new helper for durable dedup identity generation
  - prefers the exact Teams `activity.id` when available instead of collapsing purely on short text like `cancel`
- `src/bot/HelkinSwarmBot.ts`
  - added pre-ack duplicate-overseer checks so retries do not create a fresh placeholder before being deduped
  - wired `context.activity.id` through the overseer handoff path
  - if a race still dedups after the placeholder is sent, the duplicate placeholder is suppressed instead of being left stranded forever
- regression coverage:
  - `tests/bot/overseerDedupIdentity.test.ts`
  - `tests/bot/overseerDedupGuardRouting.test.ts`

## Local validation
Ran:
- `pnpm test -- tests/bot/overseerDedupIdentity.test.ts tests/bot/overseerDedupGuardRouting.test.ts tests/bot/assetIngestSelftestRouting.test.ts tests/bot/assetReplySelftestRouting.test.ts tests/bot/assetStoreSelftestRouting.test.ts tests/orchestrator/ackCorrelation.test.ts`
- `pnpm build`

Result:
- О“ВЈГ  6/6 focused test files passed
- О“ВЈГ  12/12 tests passed
- О“ВЈГ  build passed

## Deploy
- commit: `7025784`
- workflow: `Deploy Stamp`
- run: `23782680329`
- result: О“ВЈГ  success

## Live validation on deployed runtime
### Warm-up note
The first message after the deploy landed during the runtime's own startup notice window and got no reply, so I did **not** count it. I warmed the message path with `/status`, which restored runtime health to:
- `status = healthy`
- `messagePath = ok`
- `lastSuccessAt = 2026-03-31T06:03:13.236Z`

### Quoted usable-answer path
Prompt sent:
- `/light please put tea with a friend on my calendar tomorrow for issue 431 quoted resume secondary 0331E-retry`
- human message id: `1774937025496`

Clarification prompt:
- bot message id: `1774937026430`
- prompt correlation: `593dd07d`

Quoted reply sent:
- `12:30 PM`
- human message id: `1774937058450`
- quoted reply targeted bot prompt `1774937026430`

Observed result:
- the turn did **not** strand at the placeholder
- it progressed through a full orchestrator trace under correlation `1d06beee-4276-452f-a5b2-ff8326280ce9`
- trace included:
  - `BotMessageReceived`
  - `StateLoaded`
  - multiple `ClarificationResumed`
  - `PromptBuilt`
  - `LlmCallStarted`
  - `LlmCallCompleted`
  - `ToolExecuted`
  - `ReplySent`
- final bot reply message id: `1774937059111`
- final visible text:
  - `Mr. Anderson, I canО“ГҮГ–t create the calendar event from the tools available in this turn...`

Important interpretation:
- this is **not** the old ack-strand failure
- the quoted usable answer now resumes into downstream handling and reaches a terminal reply
- that downstream reply surfaced a separate calendar-routing/tool-exposure problem already in the `#400` / `#432` family, not this quoted-reply bug

Runtime after completion:
- `status = healthy`
- `pendingAcks = 0`
- `activeTurns = 0`

### Quoted cancel path
Prompt sent:
- `/light please put coffee with a friend on my calendar tomorrow for issue 431 quoted cancel secondary 0331F`
- human message id: `1774937224874`

Clarification prompt:
- bot message id: `1774937225697`
- prompt correlation: `58a0e887`

Quoted reply sent:
- `cancel`
- human message id: `1774937297800`
- quoted reply targeted bot prompt `1774937225697`

Observed result:
- the turn again did **not** strand at the placeholder
- final bot reply message id: `1774937298593`
- final visible text:
  - `Cancelled. No calendar event was created.`
- reply correlation: `6b4073bf`

Runtime/session evidence after quoted cancel:
- `status = healthy`
- `pendingAcks = 0`
- `activeTurns = 0`
- session trace for `6b4073bf-ff2f-4de1-a0c7-737509911b0e` included:
  - `BotMessageReceived`
  - `StateLoaded`
  - `ClarificationResumed`
  - `PromptBuilt`
  - `LlmCallStarted`
  - `LlmCallCompleted`
  - `ToolExecuted`
  - `ReplySent`

## Conclusion
This issue's original failure mode is no longer reproducible on the deployed runtime:
- quoted clarification replies no longer leave a stranded ack behind
- quoted `cancel` now reaches a visible terminal cancellation reply
- quoted usable answers now resume the original request into downstream handling rather than dying at the placeholder

Closing as fixed by `7025784`.

Fresh repro while validating the new attachment proof workflow from #415.

## Probe
Sent on the stable runtime:
- `/assetingest selftest secondary`
- human message id: `1774934274422`
- sent at: `2026-03-31T05:17:54.422Z`

## Observed result
- bot reply: `О“ГңГЎвҲ©в••Г… Operational note: the model provider returned an unexpected error. Please retry in a minute.`
- correlation: `43ead972`
- elapsed: `1.5s`
- no runtime-asset summary of the synthetic ingested attachments was produced

## Runtime before/after
Before:
- `status = healthy`
- `messagePath = ok`
- `pendingAcks = 0`
- `activeTurns = 0`

After:
- still `status = healthy`
- still `messagePath = ok`
- `pendingAcks = 0`
- `activeTurns = 0`
- LLM diagnostics updated with a fresh failure on `gpt-5.4-mini`:
  - `lastFailureAt = 2026-03-31T05:17:56.669Z`
  - `consecutiveFailures = 1`

## Why this matters
The new proof path from #415 is doing its job: storage and outbound attachment reply seams pass, but the upload-driven attachment-ingestion summary path still reproduces the provider-error family tracked here.

Delivered in commit `2d929c5` (`fix(#430): keep assetingest selftest text-only`).

## Root cause
The live failure was not in runtime asset persistence itself. The owner-only `/assetingest selftest` path was forwarding `ingested.imageUrls` into the overseer even though the self-test prompt only asks the model to summarize runtime asset metadata.

Repo-grounded fix:
- `src/bot/HelkinSwarmBot.ts`
